titleStmt.title: Quasiparticle scattering in a superconductor near a nematic critical point: resonance mode and multiple attractive channels

funder.orgName: Office of Basic Energy Sciences, U.S. Department of Energy

affiliation.orgName: William I. Fine Theoretical Physics Institute
William I. Fine Theoretical Physics Institute
William I. Fine Theoretical Physics Institute

analytic.title: Quasiparticle scattering in a superconductor near a nematic critical point: resonance mode and multiple attractive channels

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We analyze the scattering rate for 2D fermions interacting via soft nematic fluctuations. The ground state is an s-wave superconductor, but other pairing channels are almost equally attractive. This strongly alters the scattering rate: At energies beyond the pairing gap ∆, it is renormalized by contributions from all pairing channels. At energies of order ∆, it is determined by the competition between scattering into a gapped continuum and dispersing nematic resonance. The outcome is a "peak-peak-dip-hump" spectrum, similar, but not identical, to the "peak-dip-hump" structure in the cuprates.

Introduction.-The spin resonance mode, observed in a number of quasi-2D cuprate superconductors at momentum (π, π), has attracted much attention in the studies of high-temperature superconductivity [1][2][3]. The resonance mode mediates a 4-fermion interaction and accounts for the "peak-dip-hump" structure of the fermionic spectral function observed in ARPES measurements [4][5][6][7][8][9]. In an itinerant approach, the resonance can be viewed as a spin exciton, whose emergence below the threshold for the continuum is associated with the d-wave symmetry of a gap function (see e.g., [10,11] and references therein).

In this Letter, we argue that a similar phenomenon takes place in a 2D s-wave superconductor near a quantum critical point (QCP) towards a charge nematic order. We demonstrate that the nematic propagator possesses a charge exciton at a frequency Ω res (q), which near an instability is far below the threshold for the continuum at twice the gap value, 2∆. A nematic resonance at q = 0 has been observed in the B 1g Raman spectra of several iron-based superconductors [12][13][14][15][16]; here we show that Ω res (q) and the spectral weight of the resonance are strongly q-dependent, which is tied to the (ω/|q|)-form of the Landau damping of a nematic propagator in the normal state. We analyze how the dispersing charge exciton affects the fermionic spectral function at energies above the threshold at ∆ + Ω res (q = 0). We find that the spectral function is highly non-monotonic due to strong momentum dependence of Ω res (q). In contrast, a spin resonance in a d-wave superconductor does not lead to such behavior as there relevant momenta q are close to (π, π).

We further analyze the form of the spectral function at energies above 3∆, when the key contribution comes from the continuum in the nematic propagator. Here, we reveal additional physics of a nematic QCP. Namely, for an isotropic fermionic dispersion, the interaction, mediated by nematic fluctuations, yields an attraction in all pairing channels. The s-wave coupling is the largest one, but only by a small number, proportional to the distance to a nematic transition [17,18]. The continuum part of the order parameter propagator has contributions from all pairing channels, and the fermionic self-energy and the spectral function due to interaction with the continuum are very different from the ones expected for a pure s-wave superconductor. We single out the s-wave contribution and show that it only accounts for a small portion of the full self-energy. We show that at even larger frequencies the self-energy due to interaction with a continuum gradually approaches the normal state result, obtained by summing up series of logarithmical contributions from backscattering.

Superconductivity at the onset of a nematic order occurs in the much studied FeSe 1-x S x -nematic order at x ≤ 20% (see, e.g., [19] and Refs. within). Our results for the spectral function can be verified in ARPES measurements around the critical doping.

Model and normal state results.-We consider a system of 2D itinerant spin-1/2 fermions on a square lattice near a QCP towards a d-wave nematic order that breaks the four-fold rotation symmetry. We assume that the interaction between fermions is mediated by soft fluctuations of the nematic order parameter. An appropriate Euclidean action is [20]
div.formula: S = σ k ψσ (k)(-iω m + ξ k )ψ σ (k) + g V σ,σ k,p,q D(q)× f (k)f (p) ψσ (k + q 2 ) ψσ (p - q 2 )ψ σ (p + q 2 )ψ σ (k - q 2 ) .(1)
div.p: Here, f (k) is a form factor with d xy or d x 2 -y 2 symmetry, g is the effective fermion-boson coupling, and

where M measures the small deviation from the QCP and Π(q) is the dynamical polarization, induced by the fermions, which has to be computed within the effective action of Eq. (1). D(q) should be seen as an effective interaction which includes both renormalizations from low-energy [via Π(q)] and high-energy fermions, compare [21]. Eq. ( 1) can be derived from, e.g., a multiorbital tight-binding model, rotated to the band basis, similar to Ref. [22]. The same effective action emerges in an isotropic system near a d-wave Pomeranchuk instability. In the latter case, f q (k) = cos(2θ kq ) (θ kq = (k, q)), invariant under simultaneous rotation of k, q. The key physics is the same in both cases, and to simplify the presentation, we focus on the isotropic case and later discuss the changes introduced by the lattice. The effective interaction gD(q) leads to much studied non-Fermi liquid behavior at M → 0 (Refs. [20,[23][24][25]) and also gives rise to an attraction in different pairing channels. The model has two dimensionless parameters
div.formula: = M k F and λ = g 4πv F M .(3)
div.p: The first one is a measure of the distance to a QCP and the second parametrizes the coupling strength. In the immediate vicinity of a QCP, λ is large and the system necessary falls into a strong coupling regime. In our study we focus on the system behavior at small , but in a theoretically manageable Fermi liquid (FL) regime λ 1. To this end, we consider the double limit 1 and g E F , taken such that λ 1 (which implies g/E F , setting a lower bound on M [26]). In this situation, the pairing instability develops at typical energies of order e -1/λ , which remain well inside the FL regime. E.g., in FeSe 1-x S x the appropriate parameter window could be reached by increasing the sulfur doping x or pressure beyond the quantumcritical value, where = 0, λ > 1, which increases M and therefore increases (decreases) (λ). Our primary interest is the retarded fermionic scattering rate Im
div.formula: [Σ R (k)] ([G R (k)] -1 = [G R 0 (k)] -1 + Σ R (k), k = (ω, k)). We set k = k F , in which case Σ R (k) ≡ Σ R (ω)
div.p: . We begin by listing the normal state results, which will serve as benchmarks for our calculations of Im[Σ R (ω)] in a superconductor. In the normal state, the polarization bubble at small frequencies has a Landau-damping form, Π(q) = 2ρ|ω m |/(v F q), where ρ = k F /(2πv F ) is the density of states per spin. At the one-loop level the scattering rate has the familiar 2D FL form [27,28] Im[Σ R, (1)
div.formula: (ω)] = 2λ π ω 2 ω FL ln ω FL |ω| .(4)
div.p: The logarithm comes from forward-scattering and backscattering processes, which play a special role in 2D (Ref. [28]), and ω FL = v F M 3 /gρ sets the upper edge of the FL behavior; our analysis is valid at ω < ω FL . At higher loop orders higher powers of the logarithm from multiple backscattering processes appear. They collectively change the scattering rate to [29] Im
where = λ ln(ω FL /|ω|) and Li 2 ( ) is a polylogarithm. We plot Im[Σ R (ω)] in Fig. 1. This expression becomes
div.formula: ' -Im[Σ R ] × πωFL ω 2 ( ) π 2 3
div.p: Figure 1. Scattering rate divided by ω 2 as given by Eq. ( 5), for = 0.05. Inside the dashed region, the normal state results are no longer valid. singular at = 1, i.e., at ω ω FL e -1/λ and is invalid at smaller ω. The reason for this becomes clear once we note that the series of logarithms that transform (4) into (5) come from backscattering processes, which involve vanishingly small total momentum and are therefore the same as Cooper processes. The increase of Im[Σ R (ω)]/ω 2 with decreasing ω implies that the pairing interaction is attractive, hence the ground state must be a superconductor, and at = 1, ω is of order of an expected pairing gap ∆ = ω FL e -1/λ . This clearly shows that the apparent singularity in Eq. ( 5) is the consequence of ignoring the fact that the ground state is a superconductor. Below we start from a superconductor and show that the scattering rate is free of singularities at ω = O(∆). To set the benchmark for the calculations in a superconductor, we make two observations. First, the full backscattering amplitude contains the sum of the contributions from different pairing channels, because the backscattering process involves vanishingly small total and transfer momentum. To the leading order in , partial amplitudes in channels with different angular momenta are equal, and their sum yields the two last terms in the r.h.s. of (5). At = 1, this contribution tends to a constant 2Li 2 (1) = π 2 /3, although its derivative is singular at = 1. Beyond the leading order, the s-wave component of the scattering amplitude is the largest, and the s-wave pole in the backscattering amplitude gives rise to the first term in the r.h.s. of (5). Second, Eq. ( 5) is obtained with the logarithmic accuracy and is valid up to 1 -= O(λ). At the edge of applicability, the s-wave pole contribution is of order 2 /λ and is small compared to the combined contribution from all pairing channels, i.e., Im[Σ R (ω)] with the result that we obtain starting from a superconducting ground state.

Nematic propagator in the superconducting state.-The scattering rate in a superconductor is determined by the same process as in the normal state -a decay into a nematic fluctuation and another fermion (Fig. 2) [30]. However, in a superconductor both the fermionic propagator G(q) and the polarization Π(q) for nematic fluctuations get modified by the pairing gap ∆. The modification of G(q) is well-known [31], but the modification of Π(q) is model specific. In our case, a straightforward analysis yields

where q = (Ω, q) and G and F are normal and anomalous Green's functions. The sign of the F F term is the same as for the spin polarization operator at q = (π, π) in a d-wave superconductor [32]. Like there, the dynamical bosonic propagator D R (q = 0) then contains a resonance at Ω < 2∆, when gRe[Π(Ω, 0)] + M 2 = 0 [12]. One can view the nematic resonance as a particle-hole bound state within the two-particle gap; it originates from a residual interaction between Bogoliubov quasiparticles in a superconductor, which is attractive, like the original 4-fermion pairing interaction (1). We note that such resonance does not appear in the density-density correlator (a dressed particle-hole bubble with s-wave form-factors), as there Π(Ω, q = 0) has to vanish to respect charge conservation [33,34]. On a technical level, this holds because the GG -F F contribution cancels out by the contribution containing GF combinations and vertex corrections [34].

For the nematic correlator that we consider, there is no cancellation and the physics can be captured by keeping only GG -F F terms (see the supplementary material (SM) [35] for more discussion on this).
We present the full analysis of Π R (q) in our case in the SM [35] and here quote the results. We have, at Ω ∆,

The existence of the two regimes is specific to our case of small-q fluctuations and originates from the 1/|q|-dependence of the Landau damping in the normal state. Substituting Π R (q) into D R (q), we find that B = -Im[D R ] consists of a resonance (a pole) and a continuum, B(q) = B res (q) + B cont (q) -see Fig. 3. The resonance describes a 2D plasmon-like propagating quasiparticle whose position and the spectral weight strongly depend on the ratio v F |q|/∆. We find
div.formula: B res (q) = a q × δ(Ω -Ω res (q))(8)
Ω res (q) ∼ ∆ × /λ v F |q| ∆ ∆v F |q| × /λ v F |q| ∆ ,
div.p: and a q varies between a q ∼ ∆ at v F |q| ∆ and a q ∼ ∆v F |q| at v F |q| ∆. At small and v F |q| ∼ ∆, Ω res (q) is much smaller than ∆.

The continuum B cont (q) sets in at Ω = 2∆ (see [35] for details). The full B(Ω, q) is sketched in Fig. 3. For v F |q| ∼ ∆, the resonance is well separated from the continuum, but once v F |q| exceeds ∆(λ/ ), the resonance approaches the continuum, and its weight becomes exponentially small. Likewise, a small-weight resonance close to Ω = 2∆ is obtained for very weak coupling when /λ becomes too large. [36] Fermionic scattering rate at ω ∼ ∆. -With B(Ω, q) at hand, one can straightforwardly compute the scattering rate using the spectral representation. We present the details in the SM and here list the results and present the plot of Im[Σ R (ω)], Fig. 4. We obtain 2∆
div.formula: B(Ω, q) |q| Ω Figure 3.
div.p: Sketch of the nematic fluctuation spectrum
div.formula: B(Ω, q) = -Im[D R (Ω, q)]. Im[Σ R (ω)] ∼                              λ∆ λ 1/4 , 0 < ω -ω th ∆ 3 ω FL 2 λ 5/2 regime I ∆ 2 ω FL 3 λ 1/2 ∆ (ω -ω th ) 1/2 , ∆ 3 ω FL 2 λ 5/2 ω -ω th ∆ λ 1/2 regime II λ∆ 2 ω FL (ω -ω th ) ∆ 5/2 , ∆ λ 1/2 + ω th ω ∆ 3 + O(λ 2 ) regime III ∆ 2 ω FL ω -3∆ ∆ 1/2 , ω ∆ 3 + O(λ 2 ) regime IV(9)
div.p: Im [Σ(ω)](a.u.)
div.formula: ω th ∼ ∆(1 + /λ) ω II III IV 3∆ I Figure 4
div.p: . Fermionic scattering rate, with scaling regimes as in Eq. ( 9). Used parameters: λ = 0.4, = 0.06.

The scattering rate described by Eq. ( 9) starts a threshold ω th = Ω res (0) + ∆, where it jumps to a value that is larger than the benchmark normal state expression Im[Σ R (ω)] ∼ ∆ 2 /ω FL by a large factor ∼ E F /∆. It remains almost constant in a narrow energy window (regime I) and decreases as 1/ √ ωω th (regime II), mirroring the familiar DoS enhancement in a superconductor.

For increasing energies, the rate crosses through a minimum and grows again (regime III), which is due to the momentum dependence of the resonance position and weight. Finally, for ω > 3∆ (regime IV), the rate acquires an additional contribution from scattering into the continuum, which gives rise to a kink in Im[Σ R (ω)]. For ω = 3∆ + O(∆), the rate becomes of the same order as the normal state result and gradually transforms into Im[Σ R (ω)] from Eq. ( 5).
In Fig. 5 we plot the fermionic spectral function
div.formula: A(ω) = (1/π)Im[G R (ω)] at ω > ∆. At λ 1, A(ω) ∝ Im[Σ R (ω)].
div.p: In this situation the most prominent feature, besides a sharp quasiparticle peak at ω = ∆, is a narrow peak at ω th , followed by a broad minimum and a cusp at 3∆. At larger frequencies where the normal state results are valid, a "hump" develops. This behavior can be termed as "peak-peak-dip-hump". At λ ≥ 1, where Im[Σ R (ω)] > ∆ at ω ≥ ω th , we expect that the peak at ω th is suppressed, as in this situation plays a "peak-dip-hump" behavior, similar to the one in the cuprates [10]. Since λ ∝ 1/M , we expect a crossover from the peak-peak-dip-hump lineshape to the peak-diphump one as the system comes closer to a nematic QCP.
div.formula: A(ω) ∝ 1/Im[Σ R (ω)]. Then the spectral function dis- 3∆ ∆ 3∆ ∆ 3∆ (a) (b) (c) ω th ω th A(ω)(a.u.) A(ω)(a.u.) A(ω)(a.u.) ω ω ω ∆ A(ω)(a.u.) ω
div.p: We now analyze how the results are modified for lattice systems. For d x 2 -y 2 symmetry, the nematic form factor is f (k) = cos k xcos k y ; it reduces to f (k) ∝ cos(2θ knx ) for small k F . Such a form factor creates hot and cold regions on the Fermi surface. Our results for Σ(ω) are valid for the hot regions. The results hold with two modifications: (i) the jump of Im[Σ(ω)] at ω th is reduced by the relative width of the hot region and (ii) there exists a small but finite background Im[Σ(ω)] from scattering into cold regions, where the gap is small [37].

Conclusion.-We analyzed the propagator of soft nematic fluctuations and fermionic scattering rate Im[Σ R (ω)] of fermions in an s-wave SC state near a nematic QCP. We argued that Im[Σ R (ω)] is qualitatively different from that in an ordinary s-wave SC because near a QCP, the pairing interaction in the s-wave channel is only slightly larger than in pairing channels with other angular momenta. We showed that the spectrum of nematic fluctuations in a SC consists of a gapped continuum and a strongly dispersing resonance mode. We found three different regimes of the scattering rate, due to the interaction with a resonance: a jump at the threshold, a decrease towards a shallow minimum, and a slow subsequent increase. A fourth regime is realized at ω ≥ 3∆, when the scattering rate predominantly comes from the interaction with the continuum. At higher frequencies, Im[Σ R (ω)] gradually transforms into the normal state expression. This leads to a "peak-peak-dip-hump" structure of the fermionic spectral function. This structure transforms into the more familiar peak-dip-hump lineshape as the system comes closer to a nematic QCP and the coupling constant λ increases.

Superconductivity at the onset of a nematic order at T = 0 develops in several systems studied in recent years, most notably in FeSe 1-x S x around x = 0.2. We call for ARPES measurements of the fermionic spectral function to verify our results. Supplementary Material for "Spectral signatures of a nematic QCP in high-Tc-superconductors"

In this supplement, we present the detailed derivation of our results. In Sec. I we compute the main contributions to the polarization function Π. In Sec. II, we discuss the GF -contributions to Π. In Sec. III, we evaluate the nematic fluctuation spectrum B. The self-energy Im[Σ R (ω)] is derived in Sec. IV. In Sec. V, we compare our computation to the SDW case.

div.head: I. Polarization function Π
div.p: Consider the polarization function Π, given by the two diagrams of Fig. 6; the right-hand diagram represents the contribution from the anomalous propagators. Π is given by:
where q = (Ω m , q) and the normal and the anomalous Matsubara propagators are defined as
div.formula: G(p) = G(ω m , p) = - iω m + ξ p ω2 m + ξ 2 p + ∆ 2 ,(11)
F (p) = F (ω m , p) = ∆ ω2 m + ξ 2 p + ∆ 2 .
div.p: We first evaluate Π on the Matsubara axis and then analytically continue to the real axis. We compute the momentum integrals first -in this way we omit a constant term lim q→0 lim Ωm→0 Π(Ω m , q), which renormalizes the bosonic mass M only. We find
div.formula: Π(q) = 2ρ dθ pq 2π f q (p) 2 ∞ 0 dω m (12
div.p: The last term R originates from the nematic form factor f q (p) = cos(2θ pq ). To obtain analytical results, we will focus on the limiting cases v F |q| Ω m , ∆ or v F |q| Ω m , ∆. In the first case, we can pull out 1/(v F |q|) from the integral, and R is not important: the angular integral in Eq. ( 12) is dominated by θ pq = ±π/2, and f q (p) 2 1. In the second case, we can neglect the v F q-terms, and inclusion of R yields a prefactor 1/2: the angular integral comes from all angles θ pq . We will take these factors into account implicitly and drop R in the following.
In the normal state where ∆ = 0, for v F q Ω m we find, as expected
div.formula: ) Analytical continuation iΩ m → Ω yields Π R (Ω, q) ∆=0 = -i2ρ Ω v F |q| . (14
div.p: Let us focus on the superconducting state, ∆ > 0, and first let |Ω m | ∆, which determines the nematic resonance. We can rewrite the numerator of Eq. ( 12) as
Inserting this form into Eq. ( 12), we can extract the leading Ω m -dependence in the two relevant limits v F |q| ∆, v F |q| ∆. We obtain the retarded expressions
div.formula: Ω ∆, v F |q| ∆ : (16) Π R (Ω, q) = - ρ 6 × Ω 2 ∆ 2 -i0 + sign(Ω), Ω ∆, v F |q| ∆ : (17) Π(Ω, q) = - πρ 4 × Ω 2 ∆v F |q| -i0 + sign(Ω) . Next, we look at intermediate real frequencies Ω ∆ (Re[Π R (Ω)] is even in Ω, Im[Π R (Ω)] is odd).
div.p: The most important threshold is Ω = 2∆, where excitation of the particle-hole continuum becomes possible. The general strategy to obtain Π R (Ω 2∆) is to perform the analytical continuation in Eq. ( 12) before integration; this correctly yields the singular part of Re[Π R ], and the imaginary part can be restored using Kramers-Kronig relations. To extract the leading contribution, we can let Ω → 2∆, ωm → 0 in all regular parts of the integral. Rescaling x = ω m /∆, we find where
Consider first the limit v F |q| ∆, Ω. Then, for α 1:
div.formula: Re[Π R (Ω, q)] = - 2ρ∆ v F |q| ln v F |q| (|Ω -2∆|) , (20
div.p: and the corresponding imaginary part reads
In the limit v F |q| ∆, the continuum has additional structure, but this is of somewhat academic interest. For completeness, we give an order-of-magnitude estimate as obtained from Eq. ( 18):
The 1/(Ω -2∆) divergence in Eq. ( 23) agrees with the (q = 0)-evaluation of Π [12]. At the largest frequencies Ω ∆, we recover the normal state result.
div.formula: A sketch of Π R (Ω, v F |q| ∆) is shown in Fig. 7; Π R (Ω, v F |q|
div.p: ∆) looks similar. The form of Π R is also similar to the SDW case [11], but the crucial difference is the overall scaling with ∆/(v F q) which is absent in the SDW polarization function.

div.head: II. GF -contributions
div.p: In principle, the polarization Π also receives contributions from diagrams as shown in Fig. 8, which contain a combination of GF -propagators and a renormalized particle-particle vertex. A priori, these contributions are not small. Without the d-wave form factor, for external momentum q = 0 the diagrams of this type cancel the (GG -F F ) contribution derived in the previous section, as dictated by charge conservation [33,34].

When the form-factor is included, the diagram of Fig. 8 vanishes for momentum-independent (s-wave) pairing interaction, as it contains integrations over a single form factor ∝ dφ cos(2φ) = 0. In our case the pairing interaction also contains a d-wave component, for which the integral does not vanish. Evaluating the corresponding ladder diagram, we find

We do not consider this contribution further for two reasons: (i) it has the same sign as the (GG -F F )contribution, Eq. ( 16), in the low-energy limit, thus it can only enhance (and not destroy) the nematic resonance. (ii) the overall factor in ( 24) must be small, otherwise the system at T = 0 would develop a d-wave pairing in addition to s-wave. Such s/d-wave admixture without nematicity is a fairly exotic effect and will likely be suppressed once we include into consideration a repulsion between s-and d-wave order parameters.

div.head: III. Nematic fluctuation spectrum B
div.p: With Π at hand, we can determine the nematic fluctuation spectrum as
Inserting expressions ( 16), (17), we obtain the pole-like resonance part of the spectrum as
div.formula: B res (Ω, q) = a q × δ(Ω -Ω res (q)) (26) Ω res (q)    ∆ × √ 6κ 1 + |q| 2 M 2 v F |q| ∆ ∆v F |q| × 8 π κ 1 + |q| 2 M 2 v F |q| ∆(27)
a q    π 3 2 × ∆ 2κgρ v F |q| ∆ √ 2π √ ∆v F |q| 2κgρ v F |q| ∆ .(28)
div.p: Here, we have introduced
div.formula: κ = M 2 2gρ = ω FL v F M = 4λ 1 ,(29)
div.p: and also assumed |q| M . Note that the weight of the resonance peak increases with v F |q|. The above expressions are valid as long as Ω res < 2∆, which constraints the maximal allowed |q|-values to v F |q| < ∆/κ 2 . For Ω > 2∆, the continuum part B cont sets in. While we do not discuss the form of the continuum in detail, notice the following trends that are reflected in Fig. 3 of the main text:

(i) As long as the resonance is well-separated from the continuum, always Π R (2∆ + 0 + ) M 2 , thus B cont does not jump at Ω = 2∆, but sets in continuously. However, as the resonance approaches the continuum, Π R (2∆ + 0 + ) > M 2 only due to the logarithmic enhancement of Re[Π R ]. Eventually, for v F |q| ∆/κ 2 , M 2 becomes the dominant scale, which means that B cont jumps at Ω = 2∆. At these large momenta, we can approximate
div.formula: B cont (Ω, q) 2πgρ M 4 × ∆ v F |q| × θ(Ω -2∆) .(30)
div.p: (ii) For small momenta, the maximum value of the continuum
div.formula: B cont 1/M 2 is expected at frequencies Ω -2∆ = O(∆) when Π R M 2 .
div.p: The weight of the continuum can be estimated as ∆/M 2 , which is larger than the weight of the resonance by about a factor of κ. As |q| is increased, the weight goes down, as is also seen from Eq. (30).
div.formula: IV. Im[Σ R (ω, k)] for ω ∆ We need to compute Im[Σ R (ω)] ≡ Im[Σ R (ω, k)],
div.p: with k on the Fermi surface. From the spectral representation, one can derive the following general formula
div.formula: Im[Σ R (ω)] = (31) g dq (2π) 2 f 2 q (k + q/2) ω 0 dΩ π A 0 (ω -Ω, k + q)B(Ω, q) .
div.p: We focus on ω > 0. A 0 is the "bare" spectral function,
div.formula: A 0 (ω -Ω, k + q) = -Im G R (ω -Ω, k + q) = πu 2 k+q δ (ω -Ω -E k+q ) ,(32)
E k+q = ∆ 2 + ξ 2 k+q , u 2 k+q = 1 2 1 + ξ k+q E k+q .
div.p: In Eq. ( 31), we can approximate f 2 q (k + q/2) f 2 q (k) (-1) 2 = 1, since the integral will be dominated by angles such that k ⊥ q.
We start with the contribution of the nematic resonance, inserting B res (Ω, q) into Eq. (31). Then
div.formula: Im[Σ R res (ω)] =(33)
div.p: g dq (2π) 2 u 2 k+q a q δ(ω -Ω res (q) -E k+q ) .

The resonance contribution is only important for ω < 3∆, which implies that ξ k+q is at most of order ∆ or smaller. To get the correct order of magnitude, we can therefore approximate u 2 k+q 1/2 and expand E k+q in ξ k+q = v F |q| cos(φ), where φ = (k, q). Im[Σ R res ] starts at the threshold ω = ω th , where
Close to ω th , we need the low-momentum branch of B res , cf. Eq. ( 26). We get
div.formula: Im[Σ R res (ω)] = √ 6 16 ∆ κρ ∆/v F 0 d|q||q| dφ 2π (35) δ(ω -ω th -(v F |q| cos(φ)) 2 /2∆ - √ 6κ∆|q| 2 /2M 2 ) .
div.p: We have cut off the q-integral at ∆/v F , which marks the upper boundary of the low-momentum branch of B res .
The trivial |q|-integral yields
div.formula: Im[Σ R res (ω)] = (36) √ 6 16 ∆ 2 κρv 2 F × 2π 0 dφ 2π 1 cos 2 (φ) + √ 6κ∆ 2 /(v F M ) 2 × θ ∆ - ω -ω th cos 2 (φ) + √ 6κ∆ 2 /(v F M ) 2 × θ(ω -ω th ) .
div.p: First consider the limit ω ω th , then the first step function in Eq. ( 36) equals one. The integral comes from angles close to π/2, 3π/2: the constant √ 6∆ 2 /(v F M ) 2 is extremely small, since ∆ = ω FL exp(-1/λ) is exponentially suppressed compared to v F M = ω FL /κ 2 . In this limit, we find Im[Σ R res (ω th + 0 + )] = (37)

Moving away from the threshold, for ωω th ∆ √ 6κ∆ 2 /(v F M ) 2 , the step-function becomes important: it restricts cos 2 (φ) (ωω th )/∆. Since the upper cutoff ∆ enters explicitly, we no longer have control over numerical prefactors and suppress them from now on. We obtain
div.formula: Im[Σ R res,1 (ν)] ∼ κ 3 λ∆ 2 ω FL ∆ (ω -ω th ) ,(38)
div.p: where the index 1 means that this is the low-q contribution. Now we compare this to the large-q resonance contribution of Eq. (26
div.formula: [Σ R res,2 (ω)] ∼ (40) ∆ 2 κρv 2 F dx dy x 3/2 × δ ω -∆ ∆ -κ √ x -x 2 y 2 .
div.p: The lower bound on the x-integral is x min = 1. The delta-function sets
To have yx < 1 ∀x (i.e., v F |q| cos(φ) < ∆), we need ω -∆(1 + κ) < ∆. Then, to have |y| > 0 in Eq. ( 41), we must require x < x max = [(ω -∆)/(∆κ)] 2 . The y integral yields
div.formula: Im[Σ R res,2 (ω)] ∼ (42) ∆ 2 κρv 2 F xmax 1 dx √ x ω-∆ ∆ -κ √ x = ∆ 2 κ 3/2 ρv 2 F xmax 1 dx √ x √ x max - √ x .
div.p: This contribution becomes sizeable compared to Im[Σ R res,1 ] if x max 1 (but still x max < 1/κ 2 , otherwise ω -∆(1 + κ) > ∆). We can then approximate ω -∆ ωω th , and the integral yields
div.formula: Im[Σ R res,2 (ω)] ∼ ∆ 2 κ 3/2 ρv 2 F (x max ) 5/4 (43) ∼ κ 3 λ∆ 2 ω FL ∆ ω -ω th × κ -3 ω -ω th ∆ 3 .
div.p: Comparing to Eq. ( 38), we see that Eq. ( 43) becomes dominant for (ωω th ) > κ∆.

So far, we only considered the contribution from the nematic resonance, inserting B res into Eq. (31). The contribution from the continuum B cont has been evaluated in Ref. [29], see App. G within. The result is, for ω -3∆ ∆:

Thus, the continuum contribution becomes larger than the resonance part (43) for ω -3∆ λ 2 ∆. Alltogether, the self-energy at energies ω 3∆ reads Here we have also added the minor Goldstone-mode contribution derived in Ref. [29] at the smallest energies. Eq. ( 45) is equivalent to Eq. ( 9) of the main text.

div.head: V. Comparison to the SDW case
div.p: There are two qualitative differences between the selfenergies due to excitation of the nematic resonance (Ising nematic case) and spin-resonances (SDW case): (i) in the SDW case, the superconducting ground state has d-wave order, and gapless states exist at the nodes along the Brillouin zone diagonal. Therefore, Im[Σ R (ω)] generically starts at ω = 0. However, the nodal states cannot couple efficiently to the spin resonance due to a momentum mismatch (see, e.g., Ref. [10]), and therefore their contribution to the self-energy is small. (ii) the spin resonance is peaked at a large momentum Q = (π, π) ∼ k F and not at Q 0. As a result, the quadratic part of the corresponding polarization function scales as [11] Π R SDW (Ω) ∼ ρ
while in the nematic case Π R ∼ ρ Ω 2 ∆ 2 . Thus, Π R is generically much larger in the nematic case. In the SDW case, one then finds that the spin resonance is located at
div.formula: Ω SDW res ∼ ∆ω SF ,(47)
div.p: where ω SF is the analog of ω FL . For Eq. ( 47) to hold, one needs ω SF < ∆. This shows that a generic spin resonance is only possible at strong coupling λ 1, otherwise ∆ ∼ ω SF exp(-1/λ) ω SF . Without the contribution from the nodal states, the SDW self-energy then starts at ∆ + Ω SDW res . At larger energies, it becomes smaller, similar to the nematic case. However, with a calculation analogous to Eq. ( 35) one can show that this reduction is only by a factor

The imaginary part of the self-energy is therefore steplike and not a sharp peak; it looks similar to Fig. 7. Thus, only the peak-dip-hump scenario can be realized in the SDW case, not the peak-peak-dip-hump scenario.

If the coupling λ is small in the SDW case, a spin resonance is in principle still possible (see [11] and references within). This weak-coupling resonance occurs due to the logarithmic divergence of the polarization function at Ω = 2∆, and appears at Ω SDW res = 2∆(1 -Z s ), where
div.formula: Z s = exp(-ω SF /∆) (49)
div.p: is also the weight of this resonance (similar to a q in Eq. ( 26)). But for λ 1, ∆/ω SF is exponentially small, thus Z s is "superexponentially" small. An analogous smallweight resonance is indicated in Fig. 3 of the main text at the largest momenta.

figure.head: Figure 2 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 2. Fermionic self-energy from excitation of a nematic fluctuation. Dashed wavy lines indicate a bare (static) nematic propagator.

figure.head: Figure 5 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 5. Fermionic spectral function A(ω) with same parameters as in Fig.4. (a) A(ω) for ω 3∆, showing the "peakpeak-dip" structure. In the inset, A is broadened with a phenomenological quasiparticle width Γ = 0.2λ exp(-1/λ), due to additional processes such as impurity scattering. (b) A(ω) at larger frequencies, showing the "hump". (c) Sketch of A for ω 3∆, Γ > 0 and strong coupling λ > 1, showing conventional "peak-dip-hump" features.

figure.head: Figure 6 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 6. Bubble diagrams contributing to the polarization function Π.

figure.head: Figure 7 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 7. Sketch of Π(Ω, vF |q| ∆). The dashed line indicates the normal state result for -Im[Π R (Ω, vF |q| ∆)].

figure.head: Figure 8 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 8. Polarization diagram containing a renormalized particle-particle vertex.
∆Ω SDW res (v F M ) 2 = O(1) for λ = O(1) .
Rescaling momenta, x = v F |q|/∆, and linearizing the angular integral around π/2 (variable y), we can write Im

note.p: F |q| κρ δ(ωκ ∆v F |q| -E k+q ) .To find the low-energy result, we require that v F |q| ∆, but still v F |q| cos(φ) ∆, s.t. we can expand E k+q as before.

figure.figDesc: 2 λ∆ , 0 < ωω th κ 5 ∆ 3 ω FL 2

row.cell: κ 3 λ∆ 2 ω FL λ∆ 2 ω FL
∆ (ω -ω th ) 5/2 1/2 , (ω -ω th ) ∆ , κ∆ + ω th ω 3∆ + λ 2 ∆ κ 5 ∆ 3 ω FL 2 ω -ω th κ∆

div.p: Acknowledgment.-The work by A.V.C. was supported by the Office of Basic Energy Sciences, U.S. Department of Energy, under award DE-SC0014402. A.K. was supported by NSF grant DMR-2037654.

monogr.title: Journal of Electron Spectroscopy and Related Phenomena
For sufficiently long-range interactions (small M ), the rate may be influenced by additional collective lowenergy excitations
Methods of quantum field theory in statistical physics
For /λ > 1, the resonance condition can still be fulfilled below the continuum threshold due to divergence of Π R (Ω 2∆)
